Option 3 raises variance on a task where a single correct answer exists.

questions.text: A code-review assistant is instructed to "give helpful feedback." Reviewers complain the output is generic. The team has a concrete list of what good review comments look like: file and line reference, severity, and a suggested fix. How should the prompt change?
options: Add "be more specific and less generic" to the system prompt.
Ask the model to rate its own feedback quality and retry when it scores low.
Send each file in a separate request so the model has less to cover.
Replace the vague instruction with the explicit criteria: require a file and line reference, a severity from a defined set, and a concrete suggested fix for every comment.
questions.rationale: Vague instructions produce vague output; naming the required components converts a subjective goal into checkable structure. Option 0 restates the complaint without telling the model what specific means here. Option 1 adds cost and asks the model to judge against the same undefined standard. Option 2 changes the scope of the input but leaves the underspecified instruction, which is the actual cause, untouched.

questions.text: An engineer estimates request size by counting characters and dividing by four, then sizes a batching job right at the context limit. Jobs containing code and non-English text start failing. What is the correct diagnosis and fix?
options: The context limit applies only to the system prompt, so the system prompt should be shortened.
Non-English text is not supported at long context lengths, so the corpus should be translated first.
The fix is to raise `max_tokens`, which expands the total context window for the request.
Character heuristics are approximate and vary by content type; use the token counting endpoint to measure real usage and leave headroom for the response.
questions.rationale: Tokenization density differs across code, whitespace, and non-Latin scripts, so a fixed characters-per-token ratio underestimates exactly this content; measuring with the token counting endpoint and reserving room for output is the reliable approach. Option 0 is false, since the window covers the whole request plus output. Option 1 invents a limitation that does not exist. Option 2 misunderstands `max_tokens`, which reserves space for the response within the window rather than enlarging it.

questions.text: A marketing team runs a prompt that generates ten distinct campaign taglines per brief. They currently run at `temperature` 0 and complain the ten outputs are repetitive variations of each other. What is the appropriate adjustment?
options: Keep `temperature` at 0 and ask for thirty taglines, then pick ten.
Raise `temperature` toward the upper end of its range for this creative generation task, while keeping deterministic settings for the extraction steps elsewhere in the same pipeline.
Raise `temperature` globally across every call in the pipeline for consistency of configuration.
Leave `temperature` alone and instruct the model to "be more creative."
questions.rationale: Sampling temperature should be chosen per task: creative divergence benefits from higher temperature while extraction and classification in the same pipeline still need low variance. Option 0 samples more from the same narrow distribution, so the repetition persists. Option 2 applies a creative setting to steps that require format and value stability. Option 3 asks for an outcome that the sampling configuration is actively suppressing.

questions.text: A team builds a prompt regression suite. When a production failure appears, their habit is to rewrite the whole system prompt from scratch until the failing case passes. Over six months, previously fixed bugs keep reappearing. What practice would fix this pattern?
options: Freeze the prompt after the next rewrite and route all future fixes to post-processing code.
Add every production transcript to the prompt as few-shot examples so the model has seen each failure.
Add each observed failure as a held test case and make targeted, versioned edits to the existing prompt, re-running the full suite before shipping.
Increase the model's thinking budget so it self-corrects the cases the prompt does not cover.
questions.rationale: Regressions reappear because full rewrites discard the constraints earlier fixes added; a growing test suite plus small versioned edits preserves them and proves nothing broke. Option 0 abandons prompt-level control and pushes semantic problems into brittle string handling. Option 1 bloats context, hurts cache stability, and generalizes poorly from raw transcripts. Option 3 spends compute without addressing the missing constraint in the prompt itself.

questions.text: A document pipeline needs Claude to return a JSON object where an optional `reviewer_notes` field is sometimes genuinely absent. Downstream code crashes on a mix of missing keys, `null`, and empty strings. What prompt-side decision resolves this?
options: Instruct the model to omit the field whenever it has nothing to say, and let the parser use a default.
Specify one canonical representation for absence in the schema and instructions, and require the key to be present in every response.
Ask the model to guess plausible reviewer notes so the field is never empty.
Move `reviewer_notes` into a separate call so the main object always has a fixed shape.
questions.rationale: The crash comes from three encodings of the same state, so the fix is to pick one and require the key always be present, which makes the shape invariant. Option 0 keeps a variable-shape object and just moves the branching into the parser. Option 2 fabricates data to satisfy a format, which is worse than the bug. Option 3 adds a call and cost while leaving the absence-encoding question unanswered in the second response.

questions.text: A chatbot's system prompt is assembled per request by interpolating the user's name, the current timestamp, and a session ID into the first paragraph, ahead of 8,000 tokens of stable policy text. Cache hit rates are near zero. What should the team do?
options: Round the timestamp to the nearest hour so the prefix changes less often.
Remove the personalization entirely, since caching and personalization are mutually exclusive.
Shorten the policy text so there is less content to re-process on each miss.
Move the stable policy text to the front of the prompt and place the per-request personalization after it, behind the cache breakpoint.
questions.rationale: Caching matches on an exact prefix, so any variable content ahead of the stable block invalidates it; reordering so the invariant policy comes first restores hits. Option 0 only reduces invalidation frequency and still breaks the prefix on every name and session change. Option 1 is a false tradeoff, since ordering solves both. Option 2 lowers the cost of each miss rather than converting misses into hits, and discards policy content the product needs.

questions.text: A translation QA prompt includes three few-shot examples, all drawn from short marketing copy. In production the tool mostly handles long technical manuals, and outputs are now suspiciously terse and marketing-flavored. What is the most likely explanation and remedy?
options: The examples are anchoring style and length to an unrepresentative distribution; replace them with examples matching production inputs, or remove them and rely on explicit instructions.
Three examples are too few for the model to generalize; add twenty more marketing examples.
The model has a length bias that can only be corrected by raising `max_tokens`.
Few-shot examples must always be placed in the `user` turn, and putting them elsewhere caused the drift.
questions.rationale: Few-shot examples transfer style, length, and register as well as task shape, so unrepresentative examples actively mislead the model; matching them to production or dropping them in favor of clear instructions is the fix. Option 1 amplifies the mismatch. Option 2 confuses an output cap with a learned length prior. Option 3 asserts a placement rule that does not exist and would not explain register drift.

questions.text: An analytics assistant must answer questions strictly from a retrieved document set and must refuse when the set does not contain the answer. In evaluation it answers from general knowledge about a third of the time. Which prompt change is most likely to help?
options: Retrieve more documents per query so the answer is more often present.

State that the retrieved documents are the only permitted source, require a citation to a document identifier for every claim, and define the exact refusal phrasing to use when no document supports the answer.
Instruct the model that it has no knowledge outside the documents.
Move the retrieved documents into the `assistant` turn so the model treats them as things it already said.
questions.rationale: Scoping the permitted source, forcing per-claim citations, and giving an explicit refusal string turns an implicit expectation into a checkable behavior with a defined alternative. Option 0 improves recall but does nothing about behavior when retrieval genuinely misses. Option 2 states something the model cannot make true about itself, and it degrades honesty rather than grounding. Option 3 misuses the assistant role and can make the model treat the documents as its own assertions rather than evidence to cite.

questions.text: A team wants Claude to produce a long report with a required section order: executive summary, findings, risks, recommendations. Drafts sometimes reorder sections or merge risks into findings. What is the most reliable prompt structure?
options: Describe the desired sections in a paragraph of prose and ask for a well-organized report.
Generate each section in its own request and concatenate them in code.
Provide a tagged skeleton with one empty element per section in the required order and instruct the model to fill each element without adding, removing, or reordering elements.
Ask for the report, then send a second request asking the model to reorder its own sections.
questions.rationale: An explicit tagged skeleton makes the required order and section boundaries part of the output structure rather than something the model must infer, and it gives the application deterministic parse points. Option 0 leaves organization to the model's discretion, which is the observed failure. Option 1 guarantees order but loses cross-section coherence, since each call cannot see the others. Option 3 doubles cost and depends on the same judgment that failed the first time.

questions.text: A startup's single system prompt has grown to 4,000 words as every edge case discovered in production was appended to the end. New rules now sometimes contradict older ones, and behavior is unpredictable. What should the team do first?
options: Audit the prompt for contradictory rules, resolve the conflicts, and reorganize it into labeled sections with the general principles stated before the exceptions.
Delete the oldest half of the rules, on the assumption that the newest additions reflect current requirements.
Split the prompt across the system prompt and the user turn to reduce the size of either one.
Instruct the model that when rules conflict, it should follow whichever appears last.
questions.rationale: Contradictions, not length alone, are what make behavior unpredictable, so resolving conflicts and imposing a principle-then-exception structure addresses the actual cause. Option 1 deletes rules that may still be load-bearing without checking. Option 2 relocates text without removing any contradiction and weakens the persistent-instruction placement. Option 3 encodes a tiebreak that leaves both contradictory rules in place, so the observed behavior depends on append order rather than intent.

questions.text: A logistics company gives Claude a tool named `lookup` whose description reads, in full, "Looks up a record." The tool actually queries a shipment-tracking database and requires a carrier code and a tracking number. In testing, Claude calls it for customer questions about invoices and billing disputes, where it returns nothing useful. What is the most direct fix?
options: Add a system prompt instruction listing the question categories that should route to `lookup` and the ones that should not, leaving the tool definition itself unchanged.
Set `tool_choice` to `{"type": "any"}` so the model is forced to pick a tool on every turn and stops guessing.
Rename it `get_shipment_status` and write a description covering what it queries, its required inputs, and when it does not apply.
Lower the temperature so the model is less likely to make speculative tool calls.
questions.rationale: The model selects tools primarily from the tool name and description, which are part of the tool definition it sees on every turn. A vague name plus a one-line description gives it nothing to discriminate on, so it over-applies the tool. Naming the operation concretely and describing scope, inputs, and non-applicable cases is the fix at the source. Patching selection logic into the system prompt is a workaround that duplicates knowledge outside the tool definition and will drift as tools change. `tool_choice: any` makes the problem worse by forcing a tool call even when none applies. Temperature controls sampling randomness, not the model's understanding of what a tool does.

questions.text: An internal HR assistant has a single tool, `hr_operation`, that takes an `action` string ("get_pto_balance", "submit_pto_request", "lookup_manager", "update_address") plus a free-form `payload` object whose required fields differ per action. The team reports Claude frequently sends payloads missing required fields. What is the strongest architectural criticism?
options: The schema cannot express per-action required fields, so the model gets no structural signal about what each operation needs.
The tool has too many parameters overall, and selection accuracy degrades sharply once a single tool exceeds three top-level parameters in its schema.
The problem is that the tool mixes reads and writes, and Claude cannot reliably call a tool that does both.
Free-form objects are not supported in tool `input_schema`, so the payload never validates.
questions.rationale: A fat dispatcher tool collapses several distinct operations behind one schema, and JSON Schema in the tool definition then cannot state that `submit_pto_request` needs dates while `update_address` needs street fields. The model loses the per-operation constraint information it would otherwise use, so it improvises. Narrow tools with precise schemas restore it. The parameter-count claim is invented; there is no three-parameter threshold. Mixing reads and writes is a permissioning and safety concern worth flagging, but it is not why fields go missing. Object-typed parameters are perfectly legal in `input_schema`, so the last option is factually wrong.

questions.text: A support agent calls `get_order`, and the order ID does not exist. The engineering team currently catches the exception and returns an empty tool result with no content, reasoning that returning error text might confuse the model. Users see the agent silently invent plausible order details. What should the team return instead?
options: Nothing from the tool, but append a system prompt line saying "if a tool returns empty, tell the user the record was not found."
A tool result flagged as an error whose content names the problem specifically, such as "No order found with ID 88213. Order IDs are 6 digits; this one has 5."
An HTTP 404 status object serialized as JSON, since the model was trained on web data and will interpret status codes correctly.
A successful tool result containing a placeholder order record with null fields, so the model always receives a well-formed shape it can parse without special-casing.
questions.rationale: Tool results can be flagged as errors via `is_error`, and the content is what the model reasons over next. A specific, actionable error message lets the model correct course, ask the user for a valid ID, or explain the failure. Returning nothing gives the model no grounding, which is exactly the condition under which it fills the gap with fabrication. A prompt-level rule about empty results is brittle and does not tell the model why the call failed. A bare status code is far less informative than a sentence explaining the actual problem. A placeholder record with nulls is the worst option here because it looks like success and invites the model to treat fabricated structure as real data.

questions.text: A fintech team is adding web research to a Claude-powered analyst assistant. They need current public information from the open web, and they also need to query their own proprietary risk model, which runs as an internal service. An engineer proposes building one MCP server that wraps both a search provider and the risk model. What is the better call?
options: Build the single combined MCP server so all external knowledge flows through one auditable interface.
Build two MCP servers, one wrapping a third-party search API and one wrapping the risk model, so both capabilities are protocol-uniform and independently deployable.
Skip tools entirely for research and instead paste relevant web pages into the context window at request time.
Use Anthropic's built-in web search tool for open-web research, and build an MCP server only for the proprietary risk model.
questions.rationale: Anthropic provides a managed web search tool on the API, so wrapping a third-party search provider in custom infrastructure adds cost and maintenance for a capability that already exists server-side. The proprietary risk model has no built-in equivalent and genuinely needs a custom integration. Bundling both into one server couples an in-house capability to a commodity one and forfeits the built-in. Building two servers is the same mistake spread across two repos: protocol uniformity is not itself a benefit worth a redundant search wrapper. Pasting pages manually removes the model's ability to decide what to look up and does not scale to open-ended research.

questions.text: A travel assistant must fetch weather, currency rates, and visa requirements for a destination the user just named. None of the three depends on the others. The team wants the lowest possible latency per turn. What behavior should they design for and rely on?
options: Chain the calls deliberately, returning each tool result before offering the next tool, so the model reasons step by step over what it has already learned.
Merge the three capabilities into one `get_destination_info` tool so only one round trip is ever needed.
Set `tool_choice` to force each tool in sequence across three separate API requests.
Let the model emit multiple `tool_use` blocks in one turn, execute them concurrently, and return all results together.
questions.rationale: Claude can request several independent tools in one assistant turn, and the application is free to execute them in parallel and return all the corresponding `tool_result` blocks in a single following message. That collapses three round trips into one, which is the latency win being asked for. Deliberate chaining serializes calls that have no dependency, which is the opposite of the goal. Merging into one fat tool trades away granularity and reusability, and it hides three separately failing dependencies behind one result. Forcing each tool in its own request is the most expensive option and reintroduces the serialization the parallel path avoids.

questions.text: A compliance workflow requires that every user message about account status be answered from the live system of record, never from the model's own recollection of earlier turns. The team has exactly one tool, `fetch_account_status`. What is the appropriate control?
options: Set `tool_choice` to `{"type": "tool", "name": "fetch_account_status"}` on those turns so the model must call that tool.
Set `tool_choice` to `{"type": "auto"}` and add a strongly worded system prompt requiring the tool on every account question, reinforced with few-shot examples.
Set `tool_choice` to `{"type": "none"}` and inject the account status into the system prompt before each request.
Remove all other tools from the request so the model has only one option and will therefore always use it.
questions.rationale: `tool_choice` with type `tool` and a named tool forces the model to invoke that tool, which is the hard guarantee a compliance requirement needs. `auto` leaves the decision to the model no matter how emphatic the prompt is, so it cannot be relied on as a control. `none` prevents tool use entirely; pre-injecting status into the prompt also means the application must know in advance which account to fetch, which defeats the point when the user names the account mid-conversation. Reducing the tool list narrows the choice but does not compel a call at all, so the model can still answer from context.

questions.text: A team ships an MCP server exposing `search_tickets`, `get_ticket`, and `close_ticket`. Three separate Claude applications connect to it: a customer-facing bot, an internal triage agent, and a read-only analytics assistant. The customer bot must never close tickets. Where does that restriction most appropriately live?
options: In each application's system prompt, instructing the model not to call `close_ticket`.
In the server's authorization layer, scoped per client credential, so the customer bot's connection cannot invoke `close_ticket` at all.
In `close_ticket`'s description, adding a line saying it must not be used by customer-facing applications.
In a fourth MCP server built specifically for the customer bot that omits the write tools entirely, replacing the shared server for that one application.
questions.rationale: Model-facing text is guidance, not enforcement, so any control that depends on the model choosing to obey it is not a security boundary. Enforcing the restriction in the server's authorization layer, keyed to which client is connected, means a `close_ticket` request from the customer bot fails even if the model is confused or the input is adversarial. A system prompt instruction and a warning in the tool description are both the same category error: they are advisory. Forking a whole second server does produce a narrower surface, but it duplicates code and drifts from the shared server over time, and it still lacks per-credential enforcement if that fork is ever reused elsewhere.

questions.text: A data team's `run_report` tool returns a 40,000-row result set as raw JSON. Claude's answers degrade badly on these calls, and cost per request has tripled. The rows themselves are correct. What is the best change to the tool?
options: Return the full result set but instruct the model in the system prompt to only read the first fifty rows and ignore the remainder when composing its answer.
Return the result set as a compact CSV string instead of JSON to cut token count roughly in half, keeping all rows.
Return aggregates plus the first page of rows and a cursor the model can pass back for more.
Split `run_report` into forty smaller tools, one per report type, so each returns a narrower slice.
questions.rationale: Tool results enter the context window, so a tool that can return arbitrarily large payloads is a design defect regardless of format. Returning aggregates plus a bounded page and an explicit cursor gives the model what it usually needs immediately and a mechanism to fetch more when it genuinely does. Telling the model to ignore most of the payload does not help because the tokens are already in context and already paid for. Switching to CSV shaves tokens but leaves the underlying unbounded-result problem intact at half the size. Splitting by report type addresses granularity of selection, not payload size; one narrow report can still return 40,000 rows.

questions.text: A developer tool runs entirely on an engineer's laptop and needs Claude to read local files, run tests, and inspect the local git history. There is no multi-user requirement and no shared infrastructure. Which MCP deployment shape fits?
options: A remote MCP server behind HTTPS, so the same server can later be reused by teammates.
No MCP server at all, since local filesystem access is available as an Anthropic built-in tool.
A remote MCP server paired with a local agent installed on the laptop, tunneling filesystem and shell calls back through the network to the developer's machine.
A local MCP server launched by the client as a subprocess and spoken to over stdio, running with the developer's own permissions.
questions.rationale: MCP supports local servers spawned by the client and spoken to over stdio, which is exactly the shape for capabilities that are inherently machine-local: the developer's files, their shell, their git checkout. No network exposure, no auth infrastructure, and the server runs with the user's own permissions. A remote server would have to reach back into a laptop that is not reachable, and building for hypothetical future sharing is speculative work for a single-user tool. There is no Anthropic built-in that reads a user's arbitrary local filesystem through the API. The tunnel option is the most complex arrangement and reintroduces network exposure for data that never needed to leave the machine.

questions.text: A team is designing an MCP server for a document repository. Claude needs to (a) search documents by query and (b) have the current company style guide available as reference material that the client can attach to context without the model needing to decide to fetch it. How should these two be modeled?
options: Expose search as a tool, since it needs model-supplied arguments, and the style guide as an MCP resource the client can read and surface into context on its own.
Expose both as tools, since anything the model might need must be invocable by the model.
Expose both as MCP resources, since both are ultimately reads against the same repository.
Expose search as a tool and the style guide as an MCP prompt, since prompts are the MCP mechanism for supplying reusable reference text to the model.
questions.rationale: MCP distinguishes tools, which the model invokes to take an action, from resources, which are application-controlled data the client can read and place into context on its own terms. A search that depends on a runtime query is a tool; a stable reference document the client wants attached regardless of model initiative is a resource. Making both tools forces the model to remember to fetch the style guide, which it will sometimes skip. Making both resources breaks search, which needs model-supplied arguments and a decision to run. Prompts in MCP are reusable, user-selectable templates or workflows, not a general container for reference text, so the last option misassigns the primitive.

questions.text: An operations agent has 34 tools loaded, many with overlapping purposes: `find_user`, `search_users`, `get_user_by_email`, and `lookup_employee` all hit variations of the same directory. Selection accuracy is poor and getting worse as tools are added. What is the most effective remediation?
options: Keep all 34 tools but reorder them so the most-used tools appear first in the tools array, since ordering influences selection.
Consolidate the overlapping directory tools into one clearly named tool with parameters for the lookup variants.
Raise the model's max tokens so it has more room to reason about which tool to pick.
Add a routing tool that Claude calls first, which inspects the request and returns the name of the directory tool it should call next.
questions.rationale: Four tools that do nearly the same thing force the model into a distinction the tool definitions themselves do not clearly draw, and the ambiguity compounds as the catalog grows. Collapsing genuine duplicates into one well-named tool with parameters for the variants removes the ambiguity rather than working around it, and auditing for other duplicates addresses the trend. Reordering is not a documented selection lever and would be a fragile thing to depend on even if it correlated. Max tokens governs output length, not selection quality. A routing tool adds a full extra round trip and simply relocates the same ambiguous choice into another model call, where it can be answered just as wrongly.

questions.text: A retail company's pricing service is a stable, well-documented REST API used by one Claude application: a single internal margin-analysis assistant with no roadmap for other consumers. A senior engineer insists on wrapping it in a custom MCP server "because MCP is the standard." What is the most defensible assessment?
options: The wrapper is warranted, because MCP servers advertise tool definitions the client can discover at runtime, which a hard-coded direct API call cannot provide.
The wrapper is warranted, because calling a REST API directly from the application means the tool results bypass the model's context window.
Direct integration is fine here; MCP earns its overhead through reuse across applications or separate ownership, and neither applies.
Direct integration is fine, but only until the API adds authentication, at which point an MCP server becomes required.
questions.rationale: MCP's value is reuse and independent maintenance: one server, many clients, versioned and owned separately from any single application. With one consumer, no second client on the roadmap, and the same team owning both sides, a wrapper adds a deployment, a protocol hop, and a maintenance burden for benefits nobody collects. Defining a tool that calls the REST API directly is the proportionate choice. Runtime tool discovery is real but is not a benefit when the tool list is static and known at build time. The claim about bypassing the context window is simply false; a directly implemented tool returns its result to the model the same way. Authentication is orthogonal, since a direct integration can hold credentials perfectly well.

questions.text: A payments MCP server is used by four applications. The team needs to change `create_refund` so that `reason_code` becomes required, where it was previously optional. Two of the four applications will need code changes to comply. What is the safest rollout?
options: Make the field required immediately, since the server is the single source of truth and consumers should conform to it.
Keep `reason_code` optional in the schema but reject calls that omit it, so no consumer's tool definition changes.
Have each consuming application pin its own copy of the server so the change only affects applications that opt in by upgrading their pinned version.
Introduce the stricter behavior as a versioned variant, keep the existing tool working while consumers migrate, then deprecate it.
questions.rationale: The whole point of maintaining an MCP server independently of its applications is that the two can move on separate schedules, which only works if breaking changes are staged. Adding a versioned tool alongside the existing one lets consumers migrate on their own timeline and lets the team retire the old surface deliberately. Flipping the requirement immediately breaks two live applications at once and squanders the decoupling. Advertising a field as optional while rejecting calls that omit it makes the schema lie to the model, which will keep omitting it and keep failing. Forking a copy per consumer abandons the shared server entirely and guarantees four divergent implementations, which is the situation MCP was adopted to avoid.

questions.text: A scheduling tool takes a `start_time` parameter. In production, the model sends values like "next Tuesday at 3", "2026-08-04T15:00", and "3pm CT" on different turns, and roughly a fifth of calls fail parsing. The schema currently declares `start_time` as `{"type": "string"}` with no description. What is the best fix?
options: Change the parameter to `{"type": "integer"}` holding a Unix timestamp, since numeric types cannot be malformed.
Accept any string and add a natural-language date parser on the server so all three forms resolve correctly.
Add a parameter description giving the exact expected format with an example, and reject other forms with an error naming that format.
Move time handling out of the schema entirely and have the model state the time in prose, which the server extracts from the surrounding message text.
questions.rationale: Parameter descriptions in `input_schema` are read by the model and are the intended place to specify format constraints; a bare typed string with no description tells the model nothing, so it produces whatever form seems natural. A concrete format plus an example collapses the variation, and an error naming the required format lets the model self-correct on the rare miss. A Unix integer removes ambiguity in principle but pushes date arithmetic onto the model, which is a worse job for it than emitting a formatted string, and the value can still be wrong. A permissive server parser hides the ambiguity rather than resolving it and will silently misread relative phrases like "next Tuesday" whose meaning depends on a reference date the server may not share. Extracting times from surrounding prose abandons structured input and is far less reliable than the schema the tool-use API already provides.

questions.text: A team building a research assistant wants Claude to write and execute short Python snippets to compute statistics over data it has retrieved. They are debating whether to build an MCP server that shells out to a Python interpreter on their own infrastructure. What should they weigh first?
options: Whether Anthropic's built-in code execution tool already covers their needs, since it runs Python in a managed sandbox they would otherwise have to build and operate.
Whether their MCP server can be made stateless, since MCP servers cannot hold execution state between calls.
Whether Claude can generate Python at all without a dedicated code-generation tool being defined.
Whether to use `tool_choice` to force code execution on every turn, so that every statistical claim in the output is reproducible from a recorded execution trace.
questions.rationale: Anthropic offers a code execution tool that runs Python in a managed sandbox, so the first question is whether that built-in covers the requirement before the team takes on the considerable burden of operating arbitrary code execution themselves, including isolation, resource limits, and escape prevention. If it does not fit, custom infrastructure becomes a real option, but that is the second question, not the first. The claim that MCP servers cannot hold state between calls is wrong; a server is an ordinary process and may maintain sessions. Claude generates code without any special tool being defined, so the third option misstates the problem, which is execution and not generation. Forcing code execution on every turn would produce needless calls on turns that require no computation.

questions.text: An agent has a `delete_customer_record` tool. Product wants Claude to be able to perform the deletion end to end during a support conversation, but legal requires a human decision before any record is destroyed. Which design satisfies both?
options: Give the tool description a strong warning that it is destructive and must only be used when the user has clearly confirmed.
Have the tool perform the deletion but write a detailed entry to an immutable audit log that a compliance reviewer examines on a fixed weekly cadence afterward.
Have the tool stage the deletion and return a pending confirmation token, with the application requiring explicit human approval before the commit.
Restrict the tool with `tool_choice: none` on most turns and enable it only when the conversation mentions deletion.
questions.rationale: When an action is irreversible and a human decision is legally required, the approval gate has to sit in the application between the model's request and the actual write. A staged deletion returning a confirmation token gives the model a complete, useful workflow while keeping the destructive commit behind an explicit human step. A warning in the tool description is guidance the model may follow and may not, which is not a legal control. Auditing after the fact detects violations but does not prevent the destruction the requirement exists to prevent. Toggling `tool_choice: none` based on keyword matching is both leaky, since deletions can be requested without the word appearing, and beside the point, since enabling the tool at all still allows an unapproved commit.

questions.text: A team's MCP server exposes `execute_sql`, which accepts an arbitrary SQL string and runs it against the production analytics warehouse with a role that has full read and write access. They argue it is safe because the system prompt tells Claude to only write SELECT statements. What is the primary flaw?
options: The tool is too coarse to be selected accurately, since one tool covering every possible query gives the model no signal about when raw SQL is the right move.
The privilege the tool's credentials carry, not the model's stated intent, defines the blast radius; the connection should use a read-only role scoped to specific tables.
Arbitrary SQL strings cannot be validated by JSON Schema, so the tool definition is malformed.
SQL results are usually too large for the context window, which is what makes this design unsafe.
questions.rationale: A tool executes with whatever privileges its credentials carry, and prompt text is not an access control. Anything that can influence the generated SQL, including untrusted content pulled into the conversation, inherits full write access to production. Scoping the database role to read-only over specific tables makes the dangerous operation impossible rather than merely discouraged. Tool granularity is a legitimate concern but a secondary one here; a perfectly selected `execute_sql` is still catastrophic with a write-capable role. JSON Schema handles a string parameter without difficulty, so the tool definition is well formed even though the design is not. Large result sets are a real cost and quality issue, addressed elsewhere, but they are not the security flaw this question turns on.

questions.text: A team is instrumenting a customer-facing agent and wants to know, for each tool, how often the model picks it when it should and how often it picks it wrongly. An engineer proposes measuring only end-to-end task success. What is the strongest argument for measuring tool selection separately?
options: End-to-end success cannot be measured for agents that call tools, because the tool results are not part of the model's output.
Per-tool metrics are required before `tool_choice` can be configured correctly.
Tool selection accuracy is the only metric that correlates with user satisfaction in agentic systems, which makes end-to-end success redundant to track.
End-to-end success is an aggregate that hides which specific tool definition is failing, so per-tool data is what makes a fix actionable.
questions.rationale: End-to-end success is worth tracking, but when it drops it does not say whether the model picked the wrong tool, passed bad arguments, or handled a valid result poorly. Per-tool selection data localizes the defect to a specific definition, which is what makes the fix actionable, since the remedy is usually a rewritten description or a tightened schema. End-to-end success is perfectly measurable for tool-using agents, so the second option is false. `tool_choice` is a per-request configuration that does not depend on having selection metrics. The last option overclaims: an agent can select every tool correctly and still fail the user's task, so the two metrics are complements rather than substitutes.

questions.text: An inventory tool intermittently fails with a transient upstream timeout, roughly one call in twenty. Currently the application catches the timeout and returns the raw stack trace as the tool result. Claude typically responds by apologizing to the user and giving up. What is the better handling?
options: Retry inside the tool with a short backoff, and if retries are exhausted return a concise error saying the service was unreachable.
Return the stack trace but add a system prompt instruction telling Claude to retry any tool that returns an exception.
Return an empty successful result so the model continues the conversation without surfacing infrastructure problems to the user.
Force a second call to the same tool using `tool_choice` whenever the previous result contained an error, so failures are always retried at the model level.
questions.rationale: A transient infrastructure failure is best absorbed where it occurs, so retrying inside the tool with backoff resolves most of these before the model ever sees them. When retries genuinely fail, a short error naming the condition and its transience gives the model something useful to say and act on, whereas a stack trace is noise the model cannot interpret and often reads as terminal. Pushing retry logic into the system prompt makes reliability depend on the model's judgment and burns extra inference round trips for an infrastructure concern. An empty successful result misrepresents failure as success and invites fabrication. Blindly forcing an immediate repeat call with `tool_choice` retries even non-transient errors, such as a bad argument, which will fail identically every time.

questions.text: A company is planning integrations for a new Claude agent across four capabilities: reading the team's Google Drive (a well-maintained third-party MCP server already exists), fetching current public web pages, querying an internal microservice with a clean REST API used by this agent alone, and orchestrating a proprietary multi-step provisioning workflow that three other Claude applications will also need. What is the best allocation?
options: Build custom MCP servers for all four so the integration surface is uniform and independently versioned.
Connect the existing Drive MCP server, use the built-in web tool for public pages, call the internal REST API directly, and build a new server only for provisioning.
Build one MCP server exposing all four capabilities as tools, giving the agent a single connection to manage.
Use the built-in web tool for public pages and build custom MCP servers for the other three, since only public web access has a first-party equivalent available.
questions.rationale: Each capability should get the lightest integration that meets its actual requirements. An existing, maintained MCP server is reused rather than rebuilt. Public web access has a first-party built-in, so no custom infrastructure is justified. The internal REST API has exactly one consumer and a clean interface, so a wrapper would add a hop and a deployment for no reuse benefit. The provisioning workflow is the one case with genuine multi-application demand and proprietary logic, which is precisely where an MCP server pays for itself. Building all four is uniformity for its own sake and rebuilds two integrations that already exist in better form. One combined server couples four unrelated lifecycles and cannot be reused piecemeal by the other three applications. The last option correctly spares the web tool but still rebuilds the Drive server and wraps a single-consumer API.

questions.text: An engineer adds prompt caching to a document-QA endpoint. Each request is assembled in this order: a system block containing the current UTC timestamp and the requester's display name, then a 40,000-token policy manual, then the user's question. A `cache_control` breakpoint sits at the end of the manual. After a week in production, cache hit rate is effectively zero and input costs are slightly higher than before caching was added. What is the cause and the correct fix?
options: The manual exceeds the maximum size of a single cacheable block, so it is silently skipped; split it into four smaller blocks with a breakpoint on each.
Caching applies only to `tools` and `system` blocks, so a breakpoint placed after document content in the messages array is ignored; move the manual into `system`.
The timestamp and display name sit ahead of the manual, so the cached prefix differs on every call; move those volatile fields after the manual, below the breakpoint.
The default cache lifetime is expiring between requests; keep the ordering and switch the breakpoint to the extended one-hour TTL.
questions.rationale: Prompt caching matches on an exact prefix. Anything that changes at the front of the request invalidates everything after it, so a per-request timestamp ahead of a 40k-token manual guarantees a miss on every call, and you also pay the cache-write premium each time, which explains the slight cost increase. Ordering stable content first and volatile content last is the fix. Splitting into more blocks (A) does not help because the invalidation happens upstream of all of them, and there is no such block-size ceiling. (B) is false: cacheable content is not limited to `tools` and `system`. (D) misdiagnoses the failure as expiry; a longer TTL on a prefix that changes every request still never hits.

questions.text: An internal support assistant sends a 60,000-token stable prefix (system instructions plus a product handbook) with every request. Traffic is bursty during business hours: a few questions, then idle gaps of twenty to forty minutes, then another burst. The team is currently on the default cache lifetime and sees hits inside a burst but a full cache write at the start of every burst. Cost review flags the repeated writes. What is the best change?
options: Switch the stable prefix to the extended one-hour cache TTL, accepting its higher write premium in exchange for hits that survive the idle gaps.
Run a scheduled keep-alive request against the full prefix every four minutes, around the clock, so the entry never expires.
Remove caching entirely and move the workload to a smaller, cheaper model to offset the prefix cost.
Fine-tune a model on the product handbook so the handbook no longer needs to be sent in the prompt.
questions.rationale: The idle gaps are longer than the default cache lifetime, so every burst starts cold. The extended TTL is designed exactly for this shape of traffic: you pay a higher one-time write but hit across the gaps. (B) technically refreshes the entry but pays a cache read on 60k tokens every four minutes forever, including overnight, which costs more than the write premium and breaks on any deploy that changes the prefix. (C) trades a solvable caching problem for a quality regression and still resends 60k tokens. (D) is not an option on the Anthropic API; there is no customer fine-tuning surface, and even if there were, handbook updates would require retraining.

questions.text: A production agent ships eighteen shared tool definitions plus a long system prompt, all cached behind a single breakpoint. Recently the team began appending each user's enabled integrations as extra tool definitions, serialized from an unordered map. Cache hit rate collapsed from roughly 90 percent to under 10 percent even though the system prompt did not change. What is happening, and what fixes it?
options: Tool definitions are not part of the cached prefix, so the regression must come from an unnoticed change in the system prompt; diff the prompt across deploys.

Tool definitions sit at the front of the prefix, so a per-user and non-deterministically ordered tool list invalidates everything after it; serialize tools in a stable order with the shared block first and per-user tools last, and place the breakpoint after the shared block.
Each tool needs its own cache entry; raise the number of `cache_control` breakpoints to one per tool so a changed integration invalidates only that tool.
The cache is keyed on a hash of the entire request body, so any per-user variation invalidates it; the only fix is to stop varying tools per user.
questions.rationale: Tools are serialized ahead of the system prompt and messages, so an unordered, user-specific tool list changes the very front of the prefix and invalidates the rest, exactly matching the symptom of a collapsed hit rate with an unchanged system prompt. Stable ordering plus shared-before-variable placement restores a long reusable prefix. (A) is a false premise: tools are cacheable prefix content. (C) fails because only a small fixed number of breakpoints is supported, and per-tool entries would not help anyway since invalidation is positional. (D) misstates the mechanism: caching is prefix-based, not whole-body-hash-based, which is precisely why reordering the request works.

questions.text: A support team wants Claude to answer customer questions from a knowledge base of roughly 900 documents totaling about 12 million tokens. Answers must cite specific procedural steps, and the endpoint has a 3-second p95 latency target. An engineer proposes putting the whole corpus in context. What should the architect recommend?
options: Put the entire corpus in context using the extended-context beta so no relevant document can ever be missed.
Compress each document to a 200-token summary and stuff all 900 summaries into every request.
Fine-tune a model on the knowledge base so the corpus is absorbed into the weights.
Retrieve a small top-k set of relevant chunks per query, and cache the stable system instructions and answer-format preamble while passing retrieved chunks as fresh content.
questions.rationale: Twelve million tokens does not fit in any available context window, and even if it did, the per-request cost and time-to-first-token would blow the latency target. Retrieval keeps the per-request payload small while caching the genuinely stable preamble captures the reuse that is actually available. (A) is not physically possible at that corpus size and would violate the SLA regardless. (B) fits, but 200-token summaries strip out the procedural detail the answers must cite, so it trades a latency problem for a correctness problem. (C) is not an available capability on the Anthropic API, and weight-baked knowledge cannot be cited or updated when a procedure changes.

questions.text: A coding agent is 40 turns into a refactor. Its context is at 85 percent capacity and contains: the original task instruction and acceptance criteria, an approved plan, full contents of nine files it read, a 30,000-line build log it grepped, and its own reasoning from every turn. The next tool call will overflow the window. What should the agent drop and keep?
options: Replace bulky raw tool output with distilled findings plus the file paths needed to re-read on demand, while preserving the task instruction, the plan, and the most recent turns verbatim.
Trim from the front, dropping the original task instruction and earliest turns first, since the oldest content is the least relevant to the next action.
Drop the system prompt and tool definitions, which are the largest fixed cost and can be re-sent only when a tool is actually needed.
Keep everything intact and switch to a model with a larger context window so no information is lost.
questions.rationale: Raw tool output is high-volume and low-density, and it is recoverable: a grep result or file body can be re-fetched from a path, whereas the task goal cannot be reconstructed. Distilling it while protecting the instruction, the plan, and recency is the highest-value-per-token trade. (B) drops the acceptance criteria, which is the one thing that makes the remaining work correct, and it is the classic cause of agents drifting off-task late in a run. (C) removes the stable prefix that anchors both behavior and the prompt cache, and an agent without tool definitions cannot act. (D) postpones rather than solves: the same overflow returns twenty turns later at higher cost, and degraded attention over a bloated context is itself a quality risk.

questions.text: A B2B onboarding assistant holds multi-hour conversations in which the rep and Claude agree on configuration decisions, quoted discounts, and follow-up commitments. Sessions now regularly approach the context limit. Continuity matters: forgetting a committed discount is a business incident. What long-conversation strategy should the architect adopt?
options: Hard-truncate to the last 20 messages on every request, since recent turns are what the model is responding to.
End the session at the limit and start a fresh conversation, asking the rep to restate anything still relevant.

Compact earlier turns into a structured state block capturing decisions, entities, open items, and explicit commitments, keep recent turns verbatim, and carry that block forward on every subsequent request.
Store every prior turn in a vector store and retrieve only the turns semantically similar to the current message.
questions.rationale: A structured compaction preserves the durable state that drives correctness (decisions and commitments) at a fraction of the tokens, while verbatim recency preserves the local conversational thread. Making the summary structured rather than prose is what stops a committed discount from being smoothed away. (A) silently discards commitments made an hour ago, which is the exact failure the business cannot absorb. (B) pushes the memory burden onto the human and loses anything the rep forgets. (D) is the right tool for a document corpus but wrong for conversational state: commitments are not retrieved reliably by topical similarity, and sequence and supersession information are lost.

questions.text: A sales-quoting assistant caches a 25,000-token pricing sheet as a stable prefix using the extended one-hour TTL. Pricing is edited in a CMS at unpredictable times, sometimes several times a day. A rep quotes a price that was changed 35 minutes earlier, and the customer holds the company to the stale number. What is the right architectural correction?
options: Accept it: an hour of pricing staleness is a reasonable tradeoff for the cost savings, and reps should verify prices before sending a quote.
Derive the cache key from a content version or hash published by the CMS, so any price edit produces a new prefix and a new cache entry rather than serving the previous sheet.
Shorten the TTL to the default short lifetime so the worst-case staleness window drops to a few minutes.
Keep the cached sheet and append a short list of recently changed prices after the cached block as a correction note.
questions.rationale: The defect is that cache lifetime is decoupled from data freshness. Tying the prefix to a CMS-published version or content hash makes staleness impossible by construction: a change to the underlying data changes the prefix, which forces a new entry, and unchanged data keeps hitting. (A) is a policy dodge for a correctness bug with contractual consequences. (C) reduces the window but does not close it, and multiplies cache writes on a 25k-token sheet, so it costs more and is still wrong sometimes. (D) leaves two contradictory price lists in context and relies on the model to prefer the later one, which is a fragile grounding assumption for a number the company is legally bound to.

questions.text: An API gateway rejects oversized requests before forwarding them to Claude, using an estimate of character count divided by four. Since the team added image attachments and a large tool schema, users report both false rejections of requests that would have fit and occasional real context-overflow errors that slipped through. What should the team do?
options: Tighten the heuristic to characters divided by three to build in a safety margin against underestimation.
Use an open-source model's tokenizer locally as a close-enough proxy for Claude's tokenization.
Read `usage.input_tokens` from the previous response for that conversation and extrapolate the next request's size from it.
Call the token counting endpoint with the exact payload being sent, including the same model, system, tools, and content blocks, and gate on the number it returns.
questions.rationale: Character heuristics cannot model image tokens or tool-schema serialization, which is why the errors run in both directions. The token counting endpoint measures the actual request as the API will see it, so it is the only gate that is right on both sides. (A) makes false rejections worse while still failing on images, since the error is structural rather than a margin problem. (B) is wrong because tokenizers are model-specific and a foreign one gives a confidently wrong number, plus it still cannot account for image and tool overhead. (C) reports what a past request cost rather than what the next one will cost, so it cannot catch the large attachment a user is about to add.

questions.text: A nightly job classifies about 200,000 documents with individual API calls, all fired concurrently. It hits sustained rate-limit errors and occasional overload errors, and its current handling (a fixed one-second wait, five attempts, then crash) turns those bursts into a thundering herd that fails the whole run. Results are not needed until the next morning. What is the right reliability change?
options: Retry rate-limited calls immediately with no delay, since the limit window is continuously refilling.
Raise the fixed delay to 30 seconds and the attempt cap to 20 so the job eventually drains through the limit.
Move the job to the Message Batches API, and use exponential backoff with jitter that honors `retry-after` for anything still sent synchronously.
Treat a rate-limit error as a permanent failure, log the document, and reprocess it during the following night's run.
questions.rationale: The workload is asynchronous by definition, which makes batch submission the correct primitive: it removes the concurrency contention entirely and costs meaningfully less. Backoff with jitter that respects `retry-after` is the right pattern for whatever remains synchronous, since jitter is what breaks the synchronized retry storm. (A) intensifies the herd and burns quota on guaranteed failures. (B) keeps the fixed delay, so every client retries in lockstep and the herd survives; it only makes the job slower. (D) is not a fix but deferred failure, and it compounds: each night's backlog is added to the next night's volume until the job never completes.

questions.text: A customer-service agent has a tool called `issue_refund`. During an incident, a network timeout occurred after the refund service had already processed a charge reversal but before the tool result reached the agent. The orchestration layer retried the tool call, and 41 customers were refunded twice. What is the correct fix?
options: Have the caller generate an idempotency key derived from the originating order and turn, pass it with every refund attempt, and have the refund service deduplicate on it.
Add an instruction to the system prompt stating that `issue_refund` must never be called more than once for the same order.
Raise the HTTP timeout on the refund call so the response reliably arrives before the client gives up.
Wrap the refund tool call in exponential backoff so the retried attempt lands cleanly instead of colliding with the first.
questions.rationale: The failure is at-least-once delivery meeting a non-idempotent side effect, and the only durable fix is to make the operation idempotent at the service boundary so a duplicate request is recognized and collapsed. (B) is a prompt-level request for a guarantee the prompt cannot enforce: the second call came from the orchestration retry, not from the model deciding to call twice. (C) narrows the window without closing it; any timeout value still has a case where the response is lost after the effect commits. (D) misreads the retry as the cure when it is the trigger: backing off changes when the duplicate refund happens, not whether it does.

questions.text: A summarization endpoint issues a non-streaming request with `max_tokens` set to 16,000 for documents that usually need about 800 tokens of summary. The product requires visible output within 8 seconds. In production the team sees intermittent connection resets at the load balancer and occasional client-side request timeouts on the longest documents. What should they change?
options: Increase the load balancer and client timeouts to ten minutes and keep the non-streaming call as is.
Stream the response so first tokens arrive inside the latency budget and the connection stays active, and set `max_tokens` to the length the summary actually needs.
Split every document into eight parallel calls capped at 2,000 tokens each and concatenate the results.
Set `max_tokens` to 1,000 and have the client issue continuation requests whenever the response is cut off.
questions.rationale: A non-streaming request holds the connection idle until the full response is generated, which is what trips intermediary timeouts on long generations and guarantees a time-to-first-content far outside an 8-second budget. Streaming fixes both, and right-sizing `max_tokens` removes the long tail that caused the worst cases. (A) treats the symptom, leaves the user staring at nothing for the whole generation, and violates the SLA even when it succeeds. (C) destroys summary coherence, since eight independent summaries of fragments are not a summary of the document. (D) truncates mid-summary by design and adds round trips, making tail latency worse rather than better.

questions.text: A legal research assistant answers questions over contract documents supplied in the request. Reviewers find that most answers are correct, but a small fraction assert clauses that are confidently stated and not actually present in the supplied contracts. The firm needs every assertion traceable to a source before it can ship. What is the strongest fix?
options: Add a strong instruction not to fabricate, set temperature to 0, and ask the model to say 'not found' when unsure.
Issue a second Claude call asking whether the first answer contained hallucinations, and surface that verdict alongside the answer.
Move to a larger model, which will reduce the fabrication rate on this document type.
Enable the API's citations feature on the supplied documents so each claim carries a source span, and flag or reject any answer whose claims are uncited.
questions.rationale: The requirement is traceability, not just a lower error rate. Citations bind claims to spans in the actual supplied documents, which makes an unsupported assertion mechanically detectable rather than dependent on a reviewer noticing. (A) helps at the margin but produces no artifact a reviewer or the system can check, and temperature 0 makes fabrications more consistent, not less likely to exist. (B) asks the model to audit itself without independent grounding in the source text, and the same confidence that produced the fabrication tends to survive the self-check. (C) may lower the rate but leaves the failure mode intact and unverifiable, which is what the firm cannot accept.

questions.text: A team ships system prompt edits to a production triage classifier several times a week with no automated checks. Twice this quarter an edit that improved one category quietly degraded another, and it was caught only after customers complained days later. The team wants to keep shipping quickly. What should the architect put in place?
options: Build a versioned eval set from real production cases with graded expected behavior, run it on every prompt or model change, and gate deploys on the results plus a small canary rollout.
Require three engineers to manually review 20 sampled outputs and sign off before each release.
Ship every prompt change as a production A/B test and revert if the user satisfaction metric drops.
Freeze the prompt and pin the model version so that no change can introduce a regression.
questions.rationale: The failure is silent cross-category regression, which only a repeatable graded suite over representative cases can catch before release; versioning it and gating on it preserves the team's shipping speed instead of slowing it. (B) does not scale, does not cover categories the reviewers do not sample, and drifts as reviewers change. (C) detects regressions only after real users absorb them and only when the effect is large enough to move an aggregate metric, which the days-long detection lag already demonstrated. (D) trades away the ability to improve the classifier and does not protect against upstream drift in the input distribution.

questions.text: During an incident, an on-call engineer cannot reproduce a bad answer a customer reported. Current logging captures only the final assistant text and the HTTP status code. Some requests contain personal data subject to a retention policy, and finance separately wants to understand a jump in input token spend. What logging design should the architect specify?
options: Log full request and response payloads verbatim and retain them indefinitely so nothing is ever unavailable during an investigation.
Log no user text at all and rely on aggregate latency, error-rate, and spend dashboards for diagnosis.

Log a request ID, model, prompt and template version, token usage including cache read and write counts, latency, stop reason, and tool calls, plus a pointer to redacted content held under a short retention window.
Sample one percent of traffic for full payload capture and discard the remaining telemetry to control storage cost.
questions.rationale: Structured metadata is what makes an LLM incident reproducible (which prompt version, which model, which tool path, where it stopped) and it simultaneously answers the finance question, since cache read and write counts explain input spend. Redacted content under short retention gives investigators the payload without holding personal data indefinitely. (A) is the retention-policy violation the environment explicitly forbids. (B) leaves on-call with exactly the blindness described in the scenario. (D) makes it overwhelmingly likely the reported request was not sampled, and discarding the rest of the telemetry throws away the cheap, non-sensitive metadata that costs almost nothing to keep.

questions.text: Monthly Claude spend has tripled. A breakdown shows the traffic is roughly 70 percent short intent classification, 20 percent document QA that repeatedly sends the same 50,000-token operations manual, and 10 percent complex multi-step reasoning. Only the reasoning tier has a user-facing latency SLA; classification runs on an internal queue. What is the highest-leverage cost plan?
options: Halve `max_tokens` across all traffic to cap output spend at the source.
Route the classification tier to a smaller model, cache the shared manual as a stable prefix for the document QA tier, and leave the reasoning tier on the larger model.
Move all traffic to the Message Batches API to capture the batch discount across the entire workload.
Standardize everything on the smallest available model and add a retry-with-a-larger-model loop whenever confidence is low.
questions.rationale: Each tier has a different cost driver, so each needs a different lever: high-volume simple work is a model-selection problem, repeated identical context is a caching problem, and the small reasoning tier is where paying for capability is justified. Tiering captures nearly all the savings without touching the quality that matters. (A) does not address input tokens, which is where the 50k-token manual spend lives, and it truncates legitimate long outputs. (C) applies an asynchronous mechanism to synchronous traffic and breaks the reasoning tier's SLA outright. (D) collapses quality on the hardest 10 percent, and a confidence-triggered escalation loop pays for two calls on exactly the requests that were already the most expensive.

questions.text: A field-service agent has spent a long diagnostic conversation establishing that a rooftop unit needs a compressor replacement costing well above its dispatch authority. It must hand off to a human technician who cannot see the conversation transcript. What should the agent pass in the escalation?
options: The site identifier plus a note that the job needs manager approval.
A structured handoff: site identifier, the diagnostic conclusion, the evidence supporting it, the authority limit exceeded, and the recommended next action.
The complete conversation transcript, so the technician has every detail and nothing is lost in summarization.
A customer-facing apology together with the technician's current position in the dispatch queue.
questions.rationale: A human with no transcript access needs a self-contained brief: what was found, on what evidence, what is blocked, and what should happen next. An identifier plus a bare approval note omits the diagnosis entirely and forces a full redo. Dumping the raw transcript transfers the work of reconstructing the case rather than doing it. An apology and queue position carry no diagnostic content at all.

questions.text: A compliance prompt defines a tool named `assess_retention_risk` and also contains the instruction "assess the retention risk of each stored dataset." Developers report that Claude sometimes writes a prose risk assessment instead of calling the tool. What is the most likely cause?
options: The tool is defined after the instruction in the request body, so the model has not seen it when it processes the instruction.
The tool's input schema is too complex, so the model falls back to prose when it cannot construct valid arguments.
Keyword overlap between the instruction wording and the tool name lets the model satisfy the instruction by writing the described analysis rather than invoking the tool.
`tool_choice` must be set explicitly to `auto` before the model will consider calling any defined tool.
questions.rationale: When instruction wording mirrors a tool name, producing the described text reads to the model as having followed the instruction, which is exactly the observed behavior; the fix is distinct, non-overlapping vocabulary in the instruction and the tool description. Tool definitions are available regardless of their position relative to instruction text. Schema complexity typically yields malformed calls rather than a clean prose substitute. `auto` is already the default tool-selection behavior.

questions.text: A procurement assistant is handed a 340-line bill of materials inside a single prompt and asked which component carries the longest lead time. It answers correctly when the true longest-lead item appears near the top or bottom of the list, but misses it when the item sits somewhere in the middle. Feeding the identical bill of materials in seven smaller chunks resolves the problem entirely. What best explains the original failure?
options: The component rows in the middle were dropped by request truncation before reaching the model.
Recall degrades across the middle of a long input, so items buried mid-list are attended to less reliably than those near either end.
The model cannot rank a numeric field across more than about 100 rows without invoking a calculation tool.
A bill of materials must be supplied as structured JSON rather than plain lines for field comparison to work.
questions.rationale: This is the lost-in-the-middle effect: attention is strongest at the start and end of a long input and weakest through the middle, which is exactly why chunking the same content fixes it. Truncation would remove the tail rather than a middle band, and would not be cured by chunking the same total volume. The model can rank far more rows than this when they are genuinely attended to. Serialization format is not what governs mid-payload recall.
